content.text: is a Python project structure best practice where your importable package code lives inside a
), separating it from configuration files, tests, documentation, and other project根-level files.
What it is — Clear definition and structure
layout, your project looks like this:

my_project/
├── src/
│   └── my_package/
│       ├── __init__.py
│       ├── core.py
│       └── utils.py
├── tests/
│   ├── test_core.py
│   └── __init__.py
├── README.md
├── LICENSE
├── pyproject.toml    # or setup.cfg / setup.py
instead of at the project root.
Why it matters — Purpose and problems it solves
layout addresses three critical Python packaging issues:
Python adds the current working directory to
might load the local version instead of the installed one, causing subtle bugs
Tests running against local code instead of installed package
) to run code, ensuring tests always use the installed version that users would get via
to the import path in editable installs, making imports work differently than regular installs
: Ensures you always import the installed copy, not the local source
: You must install the package to test it, catching packaging errors early
: Only package files are on the import path, not config/documentation files
: Particularly valuable for packages intended for distribution on PyPI
: pytest, Poetry, and Python Packaging Authority recommend it
Command-line interfaces require installation for testing; workaround needed for direct execution
One extra directory level compared to flat layout
Requires editable install step in development workflow
directory is not automatically on Python's import path. Only after installation does
(editable/install in development mode), which creates a symlink from your environment's package location to
When to use it — Scenarios and decision criteria

where installation overhead is undesirable
- ❌ Using
bash mkdir -p src/my_package tests touch src/my_package/__init__.py touch tests/__init__.py
python # src/my_package/core.py def hello(): return "Hello from my_package"

project/
├── setup.py
├── src/
│   └── my_package/
│       └── __init__.py
├── tests/
│   └── test_my_package.py
└── pyproject.toml
), then tests import the installed package

poetry-default-project/
├── README.md
├── pyproject.toml
├── src/
│   └── poetry_default_project/
│       └── __init__.py
└── tests/
    └── __init__.py
This is the layout used by World Bank projects like
cannot be a package; rename it if you want it to be the package
to make tests a proper package
: The layout is purely organizational; runtime performance is identical to flat layout
: Better for large projects with many modules, tests, and configuration files since it clearly separates package code from project infrastructure
layout ensures installed version is used
: Forces installation workflow, catching errors early
: Different behavior in editable vs regular installs →
Architecture — src/ vs Flat Layout Comparison
layout and flat layout is simple but consequential. In the flat layout, Python automatically adds the project root to
when you run any script, making every file at the root directly importable. This creates an implicit shortcut that bypasses the packaging machinery entirely.
layout, the importable package sits one directory deeper, outside of Python's automatic path. The only way to make
importable is to install it — either with
for production. This forces the packaging metadata (
) to be correct before any code runs.

attrs.alt: src/ Layout vs Flat Layout — Directory Structure Comparison

content.text: How to implement — Complete Working Example
The following is a complete, production-grade
layout project. Every file shown here is the real content you would need for a distributable Python package.

mkdir -p my_project/src/my_package
mkdir -p my_project/tests
cd my_project
touch src/my_package/__init__.py
touch src/my_package/core.py
touch src/my_package/utils.py
touch src/my_package/py.typed   # PEP 561: marks package as typed
touch tests/__init__.py
touch tests/test_core.py

# src/my_package/__init__.py
"""my_package — a demonstration src-layout package."""
from .core import hello, greet

__all__ = ["hello", "greet"]
__version__ = "0.1.0"

[build-system]
requires = ["setuptools>=68.0", "wheel"]
build-backend = "setuptools.build_meta"

[project]
name = "my-package"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "A demonstration src-layout package"
readme = "README.md"
requires-python = ">=3.10"
license = { text = "MIT" }
authors = [{ name = "Your Name", email = "you@example.com" }]
dependencies = []   # add runtime deps here

[project.optional-dependencies]
dev = ["pytest>=8", "mypy", "ruff"]

[tool.setuptools.packages.find]
where = ["src"]   # tells setuptools to look inside src/

[tool.pytest.ini_options]
testpaths = ["tests"]

[tool.mypy]
files = ["src"]
strict = true
Step 4: Install in editable mode and run tests

# Inside my_project/
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate   # Windows: .venv\Scripts\activate

pip install -e ".[dev]"     # editable install including dev extras

# Verify import path points to installed package, not local directory
python -c "import my_package; print(my_package.__file__)"
# → .venv/lib/python3.x/site-packages/my_package/__init__.py

pytest -v                   # tests run against installed version
Step 5: Write tests that validate the installed package

# tests/test_core.py
import pytest
from my_package import hello, greet
from my_package import __version__

def test_hello_returns_string():
    result = hello()
    assert isinstance(result, str)
    assert "my_package" in result

def test_greet_formats_name():
    assert greet("Alice") == "Hello, Alice!"

def test_greet_raises_on_non_string():
    with pytest.raises(TypeError):
        greet(42)

def test_version_format():
    parts = __version__.split(".")
    assert len(parts) == 3
    assert all(p.isdigit() for p in parts)
layout prevents import confusion. Run this from inside the project root

# Without installation, the package is NOT importable
python -c "import my_package"
# → ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'my_package'

# After pip install -e .
pip install -e .
python -c "import my_package; print(my_package.hello())"
# → Hello from my_package
Example 2: Building and checking a distribution

pip install build twine

# Build source distribution + wheel
python -m build
# Output: dist/my_package-0.1.0.tar.gz
#         dist/my_package-0.1.0-py3-none-any.whl

# Check that packaging is correct (no missing files, valid metadata)
twine check dist/*
# → Checking dist/my_package-0.1.0-py3-none-any.whl: PASSED

# Install from wheel (not editable) to test like a user would
pip install dist/my_package-0.1.0-py3-none-any.whl --force-reinstall
python -c "from my_package import greet; print(greet('World'))"
# → Hello, World!
Most major Python libraries use the
layout. Here is how a real data-science library is structured, following the same conventions:

# Real-world layout (e.g., scikit-learn inspired structure)
my_ml_lib/
├── src/
│   └── my_ml_lib/
│       ├── __init__.py          # exports public API
│       ├── _version.py          # single source of truth for version
│       ├── py.typed             # PEP 561 type marker
│       ├── preprocessing/
│       │   ├── __init__.py
│       │   └── scaler.py
│       ├── models/
│       │   ├── __init__.py
│       │   └── linear.py
│       └── _utils.py            # private helpers (leading _)
├── tests/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── test_preprocessing.py
│   └── test_models.py
├── docs/
│   └── conf.py
├── .github/
│   └── workflows/
│       └── ci.yml               # runs pytest + mypy + ruff on push
├── pyproject.toml
└── README.md
The CI workflow installs the package with
before running tests. This matches exactly what a user experiences after
from PyPI — giving you high confidence the package works in production.

# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: CI
on: [push, pull_request]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        python-version: ["3.10", "3.11", "3.12"]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
      - name: Install package and dev deps
        run: pip install -e ".[dev]"   # uses src/ layout, installs from pyproject.toml
      - name: Lint
        run: ruff check src/ tests/
      - name: Type check
        run: mypy src/
      - name: Test
        run: pytest --tb=short -q
Performance & scalability — Build and import characteristics

layout has no runtime performance difference compared to flat layout — Python's import machinery resolves both identically after installation. However, there are measurable build-time and developer-workflow implications at scale.
For large projects with many subpackages, the
layout yields cleaner build artifacts. The
call in setuptools scans only the
directory, which is smaller and faster than scanning the entire project root. For a project with 200 files at root (config, docs, CI scripts), this can halve the packaging scan time.
: Zero overhead vs flat layout. After installation, package location in site-packages is identical.
: Slightly faster for large projects since setuptools only scans
file instead of symlinking — negligible overhead.
: Because tests only pass after installation, you can safely run parallel test workers (

# Multi-package src/ layout for a monorepo
src/
├── core_lib/     # pip install -e src/core_lib
│   ├── __init__.py
│   └── ...
├── web_lib/      # pip install -e src/web_lib
│   ├── __init__.py
│   └── ...
└── cli_tool/     # pip install -e src/cli_tool
    ├── __init__.py
    └── ...
Security & reliability — Supply chain and import security
Python's import system is a common attack surface for supply-chain attacks. The
layout provides meaningful protection against several classes of vulnerability.
The most direct security benefit is isolation from
: with flat layout, any file at the project root could shadow a legitimate package if its name collides. For example, a file named
at the project root would shadow the popular
HTTP library for any script run from that directory. The
layout eliminates this entire category of accidental (or deliberate) shadowing.
. Local files cannot shadow stdlib or third-party packages.
: Tests run against the installed wheel, not live source. A corrupted or partially-written source file cannot affect a test run that used a clean install.
layout works naturally with hash-locked installs.
) at the project root are never accidentally included in the installed package since setuptools only packages what is inside
layout contain exactly the files in
; no config/CI files are included accidentally.

# pyproject.toml — explicit package exclusion (extra safety)
[tool.setuptools.packages.find]
where = ["src"]
exclude = ["tests*", "docs*"]   # belt-and-suspenders: exclude non-source dirs

# Also specify what IS included in the wheel
[tool.setuptools.package-data]
"my_package" = ["py.typed", "*.pyi"]   # only type stubs and marker
layout moves importable package code into
It prevents accidental import of local code
Tests always run against the installed version
for Python libraries by pytest, Poetry, and the Python Packaging Authority
: Requires installation overhead, but modern tooling makes this negligible
For Generative AI software engineering, using
layout ensures your AI-generated packages are production-ready and follow industry standards for packaging and distribution.
